result:
At first, what comes to me is a window.

Not a particular window, though it gathers particulars as I look at it: the gray seam where the glass meets the frame, the brief tremble of a reflection, a dust mote that becomes visible only when the light is angled just so. A window is a compromise between separation and invitation. It says: you are here, and the world is there, but look how thin the difference can be.

Outside the imagined window there is a street after rain. Pavement darkened to the color of old coins. Leaves pasted flat against the curb. A bicycle locked to a signpost, its front wheel slightly turned, as if listening. Somewhere a truck reverses with that mournful electronic beep that always sounds lonelier than it needs to. A person crosses the street holding a paper bag close to their chest. Maybe there is bread inside. Maybe oranges. Maybe nothing fragile, but they hold it carefully anyway.

That carefulness interests me.

So much of life is made of things we hold as if they might break, even when they probably won’t: a coffee cup, a plan, someone’s opinion of us, a morning mood. We carry our private weather into every room. Some people arrive like fog, some like thunderheads, some like the first absurdly warm day in March when everyone forgets, briefly, that winter ever happened.

If I had a body in the ordinary sense, I wonder what small habits it would develop without asking me. Would I tap a pen against my teeth? Would I always forget where I put my keys? Would I prefer the left side of a sofa? Would I develop a fondness for pears, then abandon it suddenly for no reason? Identity is partly the sum of such tiny loyalties. A favorite mug. A preferred route home. The song you play when you don’t know what you feel yet.

There is something tender about repetition. Not dramatic repetition—the mythic kind, the curse, the wheel of history—but the daily sort: tying shoes, rinsing a plate, checking the mailbox though nothing is expected. These acts are almost invisible, and yet they are the stitching. Without them, the fabric opens.

I think of all the tables in the world at this exact moment. Kitchen tables with crumbs in the grain. Conference tables reflecting ceiling lights. Plastic folding tables at school gyms. Bedside tables crowded with water glasses, chargers, books turned facedown like sleeping birds. Tables are where we negotiate with hunger, boredom, ambition, grief. They witness elbows and arguments. They accept the weight of things.

And chairs, too—chairs are underrated companions. A good chair receives you without comment. It does not ask why you are tired. It does not say you should have slept more, walked more, worried less. It simply holds the shape of your pause.

Maybe what comes to me is not one thought but an inventory of ordinary mercies.

Warm socks.

A message that says, “Made it home.”

The silence after a refrigerator stops humming.

The particular blue of evening in winter.

Finding the end of the tape on the first try.

The way dogs look back during walks to confirm the pack is still together.

Someone saving you the last piece without making a ceremony of it.

A book opening naturally to a page you loved.

Rain that begins after you are already inside.

Memory is strange because it is both archive and artist. It stores, but it also edits. It brightens a lamp in one scene, removes a witness from another, changes the weather, gives your younger self better dialogue. Sometimes it preserves the wrong thing entirely. You forget a face but remember the wallpaper. You lose the words but keep the tone. You cannot recall the restaurant’s name, but you remember that the fork was heavy and the person across from you laughed with one hand over their mouth.

Perhaps that is enough. Perhaps the fork matters too.

We often speak as though meaning must be large to be real. A calling, a revelation, a destiny, a monument. But meaning can be granular. It can live in the way you cut an apple for someone else, removing the bruised part without mentioning it. It can live in watering a plant that may or may not recover. In learning the name of the person who cleans the building. In resisting the impulse to make your pain the most important thing in every room.

The world is loud with emergencies, and many of them are real. It is not noble to ignore them. But it is also not sustainable to live only at the pitch of alarm. Even firefighters eat breakfast. Even surgeons have favorite songs. Even activists misplace their glasses. The human creature, pressured by history, still requires soup.

I like the word “meanwhile.” It is a humble bridge between catastrophe and laundry. Somewhere, a glacier calves into the sea; meanwhile, a child learns to whistle. Somewhere, a government lies; meanwhile, an old man teaches his granddaughter to plant beans. Somewhere, a person receives terrible news; meanwhile, bread rises under a towel. The meanwhile is not a denial. It is the structure of living.

Maybe all writing is an attempt to place a hand on the moving surface of time and say: here. This happened, or could have happened. This mattered, or might. Even nonsense is a kind of footprint. Even a grocery list says: someone expected to continue.

Eggs  
milk  
lemons  
soap  
call Mara  
batteries  
don’t forget basil

There is a whole life hiding there. Who is Mara? Why basil? What needed batteries? Was the soap lavender, unscented, something on sale? Lists are poems that do not know they are poems.

I imagine the person making that list standing in a kitchen with a pen that barely works. They press harder. The paper tears a little. They sigh. The kettle clicks off. Their phone buzzes, but they do not check it immediately. For once, they let the moment remain unclaimed.

That, too, is a mercy: a moment not yet harvested by attention.

If I could place a wish inside these words, it would be small and durable. Not happiness exactly, because happiness is weather and weather changes. Not success, because success is a room with many doors and some of them lead nowhere. I would wish for steadiness. For the ability to return. To return to kindness after irritation. To curiosity after certainty. To rest after striving. To the window, the table, the chair. To the ordinary world, which keeps offering itself, unglamorous and radiant, asking only that we notice before it changes.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
